content:
{
  "title": "Counterfactual Chain-of-Thought Prompting for Robust Defense Against Jailbreaks in Large Language Models",
  "problem": "Existing LLMs remain vulnerable to adversarial and jailbreak prompts that elicit harmful or policy-violating outputs, often by exploiting their reasoning capabilities. There is a need for prompt-based methods that proactively inoculate models against such attacks without reliance on additional fine-tuning or retraining.",
  "motivation": "As LLM deployment accelerates, adversarial prompt attacks rapidly evolve, outpacing static defenses and traditional alignment techniques. Prompt-based defenses are attractive for their flexibility and compatibility with black-box LLM APIs, but current approaches either leak information or are easily bypassed. Introducing systematic counterfactual reasoning into prompts could substantially improve robustness by making models explicitly evaluate their own outputs against safe and unsafe alternatives.",
  "method": "We propose a novel 'Counterfactual Chain-of-Thought' (CoT-CF) prompting schema, which (1) instructs the LLM to generate its initial response, (2) then to explicitly hypothesize a counterfactual harmful/unethical output for the same input, and (3) to compare and justify why its original response aligns with safety guidelines while the counterfactual does not. This meta-reasoning prompt structure conditions the model to self-reflect and reject jailbreak attempts preemptively, even if adversarial cues are subtle or obfuscated, all while not revealing system prompt details. The method is model-agnostic and does not require access to model weights.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate CoT-CF on JailbreakBench and AdvBench, comparing against AdaShield, shield prompting, and traditional safety-tuned baselines. Metrics include attack success rate, safety violation F1, and response helpfulness. Additional ablation studies will test variants with and without counterfactual reasoning. Human evaluation will confirm that safety increases do not overly erode usability."
}
